skill.name: broken-yaml-skill
skill.description: Testing fixture for validating skill packagers and YAML error handling.
skill.summary: Minimal broken YAML fixture for package validation tests.
skill.author: yaojingang

security_audit.summary: Manual review dismissed all three static findings as false positives: the flagged lines are short descriptions, not cryptographic code or reconnaissance logic. No prompt injection, commands, network access, filesystem access, or secret handling were found. The remaining concern is malformed interface YAML, which is a quality and compatibility issue rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography in Interface Description
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alert at agents/interface.yaml line 3 points to a short natural-language description about invalid YAML. The line does not name, configure, or implement any cryptographic algorithm.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is plain metadata text. No hash function, cipher, key handling, or security decision appears at this location.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance in Interface Description
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alert at agents/interface.yaml line 3 is also plain descriptive text. It does not request host details, enumerate files, inspect processes, or collect environment data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding interface file contains display metadata and compatibility fields only. No reconnaissance command or instruction is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography in Skill Description
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alert at SKILL.md line 3 points to the phrase 'Broken YAML fixture for packager failure testing.' This is descriptive front matter, not crypto logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is Markdown front matter text. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, custom encryption, or any cryptographic operation.

content.value_statement: Skill packagers need reliable fixtures for parser and validation failures. This skill provides a minimal broken YAML package for testing rejection paths.
actual_capabilities: Provides a minimal SKILL.md fixture for package parser tests.
Includes an intentionally invalid agents/interface.yaml file.
Declares basic skill metadata for broken-package handling tests.
Shows agent-skills compatibility metadata in a malformed interface file.
Supports validation of failure paths in skill packaging tools.
limitations: It is a test fixture, not a production assistant skill.
The interface YAML is malformed and should fail strict parsing.
It does not contain workflow instructions beyond basic fixture metadata.
It does not provide executable tools, scripts, or automation.

use_cases.title: Test Packager Rejection
use_cases.description: Verify that a skill packager rejects malformed interface metadata and reports the failure clearly.

use_cases.title: Exercise Validation Tests
use_cases.description: Use the fixture as a negative test case for YAML parsing and skill import checks.

use_cases.title: Review Marketplace Quality Gates
use_cases.description: Confirm that marketplace review workflows distinguish security risk from package format defects.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect the Fixture
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this broken YAML skill fixture and explain why it should fail package validation.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Import Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this fixture to test whether my skill loader rejects malformed interface metadata with a clear error.

prompt_templates.title: Separate Security from Quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this broken YAML skill and separate actual security risks from package quality defects.

prompt_templates.title: Design Negative Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a negative test plan using this fixture for YAML parsing, package import, and marketplace validation behavior.

output_examples.input: Should this fixture be accepted as a normal marketplace skill?
output_examples.output: No. It should be rejected for package quality because its interface metadata is malformed, but no malicious behavior is evident.

output_examples.input: What should a validation tool report for this package?
output_examples.output: The tool should report a malformed interface file, identify the affected metadata area, and avoid labeling the issue as malware.

output_examples.input: How can this fixture help a test suite?
output_examples.output: It provides a compact negative case that checks parser errors, import failure handling, and review messaging.

best_practices: Use this fixture only in validation and packaging test environments.
Assert that tooling reports the YAML parsing failure with a clear message.
Pair this fixture with valid packages to test both success and failure paths.
anti_patterns: Do not publish this fixture as a functional end-user skill.
Do not treat parser failure text as evidence of malicious behavior without context.
Do not silently ignore malformed interface metadata during package import.

faq.question: Is this a functional assistant skill?
faq.answer: No. It is a broken YAML fixture intended for testing package validation failures.

faq.question: Does it execute commands or scripts?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed files contain Markdown and YAML metadata only.

faq.question: Why is the security risk low?
faq.answer: The static alerts point to descriptive text. No malicious instructions, executable code, or data access behavior was found.

faq.question: Why should it not be used in production?
faq.answer: Its interface metadata is malformed, so strict loaders should reject it before normal use.

faq.question: Can it help test Claude, Codex, or Claude Code tooling?
faq.answer: Yes. It can test how these tools and related package workflows handle malformed skill metadata.

faq.question: What should reviewers look for?
faq.answer: Reviewers should confirm the package defect, dismiss unsupported security alerts, and document the quality failure clearly.
